name: Orange Free State (1856–1902)

colors.symbolism: alternating with orange in the seven-stripe field
the House of Orange-Nassau and the republic's name
from the Dutch tricolour canton — the Republic's ties to the Netherlands

description: Seven equal horizontal stripes alternating white and orange — white at the top and bottom, with three orange stripes between four white ones — with a Dutch tricolour (red over white over blue) in the upper-hoist canton.
history: Designed by King William III of the Netherlands and presented to the Oranje Vrijstaat (Orange Free State) in 1855; adopted as the republic's flag on 23 February 1856 and officially used from 1857 until British annexation in 1902. The orange of the field referenced both the republic's name and the House of Orange-Nassau; the Dutch tricolour canton signalled the Boer settlers' Netherlands ancestry. The design was revived as one of the three small flags embedded in the central stripe of the 1928–1994 South African flag.

features.symbolism: the alternating white-and-orange field, loosely modelled on the United States stripes, with the orange referencing the House of Orange-Nassau and the republic's name
the Netherlands heritage of the Free State Boers
